ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for silken tofu dessert with toppings; closest match is the general tofu food product class. Toppings (grass jelly, pearls, syrups) are not individually represented.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers soya bean curd (tofu) as a base; the composite dessert with assorted toppings is not individually coded. Closest parent match used for the primary tofu component.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for silken tofu dessert with toppings as a composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of beancurd with toppings is approximately 250 g (one bowl), consisting of silken tofu pudding with a selection of toppings such as grass jelly, tapioca pearls, or fresh fruit, and a drizzle of sugar syrup.

nutrient_source.title: Beancurd with toppings — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 'Beancurd with toppings' (cr_id F02030004). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical entry at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Tofu (soybean curd) and soy-based dessert GI — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for 'beancurd with toppings' as a composite dish. GI of 40 is estimated based on the GI of silken tofu / soy-based pudding (GI ~15–30 for plain tofu) combined with the effect of added sugar syrup and tapioca pearls (GI ~55–70), weighted by approximate proportional contribution in a typical serving. The composite estimate of GI 40 (low category) is consistent with the low carbohydrate density (5 g/100g) and the moderating effect of soy protein and dietary fibre. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (40 × 5.0 g carbohydrate per 100g × 2.5) / 100 = 5.0 g, which is low. Analogous estimate documented per Veritas source policy.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (13 mg/100g) and low sugar (2.7 g/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Beancurd with toppings is suitable for individuals with hypertension given its very low sodium content of 13 mg/100g; however, patients should request minimal added sugar syrup, as sweetened toppings such as red bean paste or longan syrup can substantially increase sugar and caloric load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (5 g/100g), low GI (~40), moderated by soy protein (2.5 g/100g) and dietary fibre (1.9 g/100g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Beancurd with toppings has a low estimated glycemic index (~40) and low carbohydrate density (5 g/100g), with soy protein and dietary fibre further moderating postprandial glucose response; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should choose plain or minimal-syrup versions and avoid high-sugar toppings such as sweetened red bean paste or longan in syrup.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (~0.2 g/100g), low sodium (13 mg/100g), plant-based soy protein with isoflavones
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Beancurd with toppings is a heart-friendly dessert option due to its very low saturated fat (~0.2 g/100g), low sodium (13 mg/100g), and soy isoflavone content from silken tofu, which is associated with modest LDL-cholesterol reduction; patients with cardiovascular disease should limit added sugar syrups to keep total sugar intake low.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Soy isoflavones with mild anti-inflammatory properties; very low saturated fat and sodium
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Silken tofu provides soy isoflavones with mild anti-inflammatory and phytoestrogenic properties; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should minimise sweetened toppings as refined sugar can promote pro-inflammatory mediators.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beancurd with toppings (豆腐花配料) is a popular Chinese-Singaporean dessert consisting of silken soybean curd (douhua or tau huay) served in a bowl and garnished with a variety of toppings such as grass jelly (cincau), tapioca pearls, fresh fruit, red bean paste, or flavoured sugar syrups including ginger, pandan, or longan syrup. The base is made by coagulating fresh soy milk with a setting agent such as gypsum powder or glucono delta-lactone, producing a smooth, silky, and delicate pudding with a mild, slightly sweet soy flavour and a soft, melt-in-the-mouth texture. It is served warm or chilled depending on preference. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 42 kcal, 2.5 g protein, 1.34 g fat, 5.0 g carbohydrate, 2.7 g sugar, 1.9 g dietary fibre, and only 13 mg sodium, making it one of the lower-calorie dessert options in the Singaporean hawker repertoire. The soy protein content contributes isoflavones with mild phytoestrogenic and anti-inflammatory properties. The estimated glycemic index is approximately 40 (low category), moderated by soy protein and dietary fibre. It is suitable for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular disease when consumed with minimal added syrup. It is plant-based, dairy-free, and gluten-free, making it broadly suitable across dietary preferences. Commonly found at hawker centres, dessert stalls, and traditional Chinese dessert shops throughout Singapore and Malaysia.
common_user_queries: Is tau huay suitable for diabetics?
Can I eat tofu dessert if I have high blood pressure?
What are the toppings in tau fu fa?
Is douhua good for heart health?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F02030004) at confidence 0.95, which is the canonical primary source for Singaporean food records. All core macros and secondary nutrients are present and internally consistent. GI is an analogous estimate documented per policy.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile (monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, trans fat), full micronutrient panel (vitamins B1, B2, B3, B6, B12, folate, vitamins C, A, D, E), mineral panel (potassium, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium), cholesterol, and moisture data for this composite dessert (beancurd with assorted toppings) are not individually reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F02030004 as of 2026. The HPB Foodo database for this entry reports only energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, and iron. No supplementary approved source (USDA FoodData Central, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA) provides a sufficiently close composite match for the remaining fields. Saturated fat of 0.2 g/100g is estimated from USDA FoodData Central silken tofu data (NDB 16126) as a supporting reference for the fat fraction, consistent with the HPB total fat value of 1.34 g/100g. The 'keto' dietary tag present in the input has been removed as beancurd with toppings contains 5 g/100g carbohydrate and typical toppings including sugar syrup and tapioca pearls are not compatible with a ketogenic dietary pattern. GI of 40 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026.

q1_food_identity.notes: Canonical English name 'Beancurd with Toppings' is precise and unambiguous. All mandatory Singaporean local-language names are present: Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal (42.0), protein_g (2.5), fat_g (1.34), carbohydrate_g (5.0) all present, numeric, and sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo (cr_id F02030004, confidence 0.95). nutrient_source fully documented.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (1.9), sodium_mg (13.0), saturated_fat_g (0.2), sugar_g (2.7) all present as numeric values. saturated_fat_g estimated from USDA FoodData Central silken tofu as supporting reference, documented in notes.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index (40), gi_category ('low'), glycemic_load_per_serving (5.0), and gi_source all present. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 peer-reviewed journal, documented with full estimation rationale.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn approximate match to tofu food product class documented with gap note. FoodEx2 code A00LC (soya bean curd) used as approximate anchor with gap note explaining composite dish limitation. SNOMED CT null with gap note. No codes invented.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well above the 200-character minimum. Covers food identity, preparation method, taste and texture, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutrient profile.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (low concern), Type 2 Diabetes (low concern, machine key type2_diabetes, human name 'Type 2 Diabetes'), Cardiovascular Disease (low concern). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence.

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: aliases array contains 14 entries covering English variants, Chinese script, Malay, Tamil, Hindi, and romanized forms. aliases_by_language populated for all mandatory Singaporean languages. Self-check: all strings in aliases_by_language verified to appear verbatim in flat aliases array — confirmed consistent. Romanized entries (Douhua with toppings, Tau huay with toppings, Tau fu fa with toppings, Dòufu huā pèi liào) present in both romanized sub-array and flat aliases array.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Built full RESULT JSON from input, added multilingual names, ontology, health context, GI estimate, and LLM description.
Iteration 2: Fixed ontology match_type values from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent'; ensured -1 values were documented in notes field.

Iteration 3: Expanded notes field to explicitly document every -1 field with the specific reason data is unavailable (HPB Foodo F02030004 does not report these fields) and confirmed no approved supplementary source provides a sufficiently close composite match, fully satisfying g3_numeric_hygiene requirements per the negative sentinel values rule.
veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: Record passes all six hard gates and scores 8/8 on the Q1–Q8 scorecard. The g3_numeric_hygiene failure from iteration 2 has been resolved by expanding the notes field to explicitly list every field set to -1, state the reason data is unavailable (HPB Foodo F02030004 does not report these fields), and cite the source consulted that confirmed unavailability. Nutrient data sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo (canonical primary source, cr_id F02030004). All mandatory Singaporean multilingual names present. GI is an analogous estimate from an approved peer-reviewed source, fully documented. Ontology anchors are honest closest-parent matches with gap notes. Health context covers all three required conditions with specific clinical guidance. LLM description is comprehensive. Aliases are consistent between flat array and structured object.
